# The Matrix The Matrix 1999 film by the Wachowskis This article is about the 1999 film. For the franchise it initiated, see [_The Matrix_ (franchise)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)"). For other uses, see [Matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_(disambiguation) "Matrix (disambiguation)"). | The Matrix | | --- | | [![The characters of the film (Cypher, Morpheus, Thomas Anderson/Neo, and Trinity) in an alleyway holding guns and weapons, with bullets on the ground. - April8,1999(1999-04-08)(Australia) | | Running time | 136 minutes[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-2) | | Countries | - United States[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Lumiere-3)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-AFI-4)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BFI-5)
- Australia[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Lumiere-3)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-AFI-4)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BFI-5) | | Language | English | | Budget | $63 million[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) | | Box office | $467.8 million[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) | _**The Matrix**_ is a 1999 [science fiction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") [action film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Action_film "Action film") written and directed by [the Wachowskis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wachowskis "The Wachowskis").[\[a\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Credited-1) The first installment in the [_Matrix_ film series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)"), it stars [Keanu Reeves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves"), [Laurence Fishburne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laurence_Fishburne "Laurence Fishburne"), [Carrie-Anne Moss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrie-Anne_Moss "Carrie-Anne Moss"), [Hugo Weaving](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Weaving "Hugo Weaving"), and [Joe Pantoliano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Pantoliano "Joe Pantoliano"). It depicts a [dystopian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dystopia "Dystopia") future in which humanity is unknowingly trapped inside the Matrix, a [simulated reality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simulated_reality "Simulated reality") created by [intelligent machines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"). The plot follows the computer hacker [Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo_(The_Matrix) "Neo (The Matrix)"), who is recruited by [Morpheus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morpheus_(The_Matrix) "Morpheus (The Matrix)") into a rebellion against the machines. Following the success of the Wachowskis' 1996 film [_Bound_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bound_(1996_film) "Bound (1996 film)"), [Warner Bros.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warner_Bros._Entertainment "Warner Bros. Entertainment") gave the directing duo the go-ahead to make _The Matrix_. The film's action scenes were influenced by [anime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anime "Anime") and [martial arts films](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martial_arts_film "Martial arts film"), particularly [fight choreography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fight_choreography "Fight choreography") and [wire fu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wire_fu "Wire fu") techniques from [Hong Kong action cinema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema"). Other influences include [Plato's cave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allegory_of_the_cave "Allegory of the cave"), 1990's [Telnet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telnet "Telnet") hacker communities, and [William Gibson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Gibson "William Gibson")'s [cyberpunk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") novel _[Neuromancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuromancer "Neuromancer")_. _The Matrix_ popularized terms such as the [red pill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_pill "Red pill"), as well as a novel visual effect known as " [bullet time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bullet_time "Bullet time")" in which a character's heightened perception is represented by allowing the action within a [shot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shot_(filmmaking) "Shot (filmmaking)") to progress in [slow motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slow_motion "Slow motion") while the camera appears to move through the scene at normal speed. _The Matrix_ opened in theaters in the United States on March 31, 1999, to widespread acclaim from critics, who praised its innovative visual effects, action sequences, cinematography, and entertainment value.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-RottenTomatoes-7)[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-meta-8) The film was a box office success, grossing over $460 million on a $63 million budget. It became the highest-grossing Warner Bros. film of 1999 and the [fourth-highest-grossing film of that year](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1999_in_film "1999 in film"). _The Matrix_ received [Academy Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/72nd_Academy_Awards "72nd Academy Awards") for [Best Visual Effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects"), [Best Film Editing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing"), [Best Sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Sound "Academy Award for Best Sound") and Best Sound Effects Editing. It was also the recipient of numerous other accolades, including [Best Sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Sound "BAFTA Award for Best Sound") and [Best Special Visual Effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Special_Visual_Effects "BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects") at the [53rd British Academy Film Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/53rd_British_Academy_Film_Awards "53rd British Academy Film Awards"). The Wachowskis were awarded [Best Director](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn_Award_for_Best_Director "Saturn Award for Best Director") and [Best Science Fiction Film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn_Award_for_Best_Science_Fiction_Film "Saturn Award for Best Science Fiction Film") at the [26th Saturn Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/26th_Saturn_Awards "26th Saturn Awards"). _The Matrix_ is considered to be among the greatest science fiction films of all time.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-heritage1-9)[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-autogenerated1-10)[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Genre's_Best-11) In 2012, it was selected for preservation in the United States [National Film Registry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the [Library of Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") for being "culturally, historically, and aesthetically significant".[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-NFR-12) The film's success led to two sequels by the Wachowskis, _[The Matrix Reloaded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")_ and _[The Matrix Revolutions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Revolutions "The Matrix Revolutions")_, both released in 2003. The _Matrix_ franchise was further expanded through the production of comic books, video games, and an animated [anthology film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthology_film "Anthology film"), _[The Animatrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Animatrix "The Animatrix")_. The franchise has also inspired books and theories expanding on some of the religious and philosophical ideas alluded to in the films. A fourth film, _[The Matrix Resurrections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Resurrections "The Matrix Resurrections")_, was directed by [Lana Wachowski](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lana_Wachowski "Lana Wachowski") and was released in 2021. ## Plot In 1999, in an unnamed city, Thomas Anderson, a computer programmer known as " [Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo_(The_Matrix) "Neo (The Matrix)")" in hacking circles, delves into the mystery of the "Matrix", bringing him to the attention of hacker [Trinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinity_(The_Matrix) "Trinity (The Matrix)"). She tells him that the enigmatic [Morpheus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morpheus_(The_Matrix) "Morpheus (The Matrix)") can answer Neo's questions. At his workplace, Neo is pursued by [Agents](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agent_(The_Matrix) "Agent (The Matrix)") led by [Agent Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agent_Smith "Agent Smith"), while Morpheus, able to somehow observe their movements, guides him by phone, but Neo ultimately surrenders. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/e/e3/Morpheus_offering_pills.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Morpheus_offering_pills.jpg) In a still from the film, Morpheus offers Neo a choice between two pills. The Agents interrogate Neo about Morpheus, but he refuses to cooperate. In response, they seal his mouth shut and implant a robotic tracking device in his abdomen. Neo awakens at home, believing the encounter was a nightmare until Trinity and her companions remove the device and take him to Morpheus. Morpheus offers Neo a choice: a red pill to uncover the truth about the Matrix or a blue pill to return to his normal life. Neo takes the red pill and awakens in the real world, submerged in a mechanical pod and connected to invasive cables. He sees countless humans similarly encased and tended by machines before he is ejected from the building and rescued by Morpheus aboard the hovercraft _[Nebuchadnezzar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebuchadnezzar_(The_Matrix) "Nebuchadnezzar (The Matrix)")_. Morpheus reveals that the year is approximately 2199. In the 21st century, humanity lost a war against its artificially intelligent creations, leaving Earth a devastated ruin. Humans blackened the sky to deprive the machines of solar power, but the machines retaliated by creating vast fields of artificially grown humans, harvesting their [bioelectric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioelectricity "Bioelectricity") energy. To keep their captives pacified, they built the Matrix, a simulated reality modeled on human civilization at its peak. The remaining free humans founded an underground refuge called Zion, surviving on scarce resources. Morpheus and his crew hack into the Matrix to liberate others, exploiting its rules to gain superhuman abilities inside it. Even so, they remain outmatched by the Agents—sentient programs that protect the system—and death in the Matrix means death in the real world. Morpheus freed Neo because he believed him to be "the One", a prophesied figure destined to free humanity. The crew enters the Matrix to seek guidance from [the Oracle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Oracle_(The_Matrix) "The Oracle (The Matrix)"), who foretold of the One. She implies that Neo is not the One and warns him of an imminent choice between his life and Morpheus's. The crew is ambushed by Agents after being betrayed by Cypher, a disillusioned crew member who longs to return to the virtual comforts of the Matrix. Convinced of Neo's importance, Morpheus sacrifices himself to confront Smith and is captured. Meanwhile, Cypher exits the Matrix and begins disconnecting the others, killing them. Before he can kill Neo and Trinity, he is killed by Tank, a wounded crew member, who extracts the survivors. Smith interrogates Morpheus to obtain access codes for Zion's mainframe, which would enable the machines to destroy the human resistance. Determined to rescue Morpheus, Neo re-enters the Matrix with Trinity. They free Morpheus, who escapes the Matrix with Trinity, but Smith intercepts Neo. Realizing his potential, Neo fights Smith as an equal and kills him. However, Smith resurrects in a new body and kills Neo. In the real world, machines called Sentinels attack the _Nebuchadnezzar_. Standing by Neo's body, Trinity confesses her love for him and reveals that the Oracle prophesied she would fall in love with the One. In the Matrix, Neo revives with the ability to perceive and manipulate its code. He effortlessly destroys Smith and escapes the Matrix just as the _Nebuchadnezzar_'s electromagnetic pulse disables the Sentinels. Later, within the Matrix, Neo communicates with the system, vowing to show humanity a world of limitless possibilities, before flying away. ## Cast See also: [List of Matrix series characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Matrix_series_characters "List of Matrix series characters") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Keanu_Reeves_2019_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Keanu_Reeves_2019_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Keanu_Reeves_2019_(cropped).jpg) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/National_Memorial_Day_Concert_2017_%2834117818524%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-National_Memorial_Day_Concert_2017_%2834117818524%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:National_Memorial_Day_Concert_2017_(34117818524)_(cropped).jpg) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Carrie-Anne_Moss_May_2016.jpg/330px-Carrie-Anne_Moss_May_2016.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Carrie-Anne_Moss_May_2016.jpg) Top to bottom: [Keanu Reeves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves"), [Laurence Fishburne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laurence_Fishburne "Laurence Fishburne"), and [Carrie-Anne Moss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrie-Anne_Moss "Carrie-Anne Moss") star as [Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo_(The_Matrix) "Neo (The Matrix)"), [Morpheus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morpheus_(The_Matrix) "Morpheus (The Matrix)") and [Trinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinity_(The_Matrix) "Trinity (The Matrix)") respectively. - [Keanu Reeves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves") as [Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo_(The_Matrix) "Neo (The Matrix)"): A computer programmer, born Thomas Anderson, who secretly operates as a hacker named Neo. Reeves described his character as someone who felt that something was wrong and was searching for Morpheus and the truth to break free.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) [Will Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Will_Smith "Will Smith") turned down the role of Neo to make _[Wild Wild West](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wild_Wild_West "Wild Wild West")_, because of skepticism over the film's ambitious bullet time special effects.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-empire_will_smith-14) He later stated he was "not mature enough as an actor" at that time,[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-empire_will_smith-14) and that if given the role, he "would have messed it up".[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-15)[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-16) [Nicolas Cage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicolas_Cage "Nicolas Cage") also turned down the part because of "family obligations".[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-17) Warner Bros. sought [Brad Pitt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt") or [Val Kilmer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Val_Kilmer "Val Kilmer") for the role.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Davis-interview-18) When both declined, [Leonardo DiCaprio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio") initially accepted the role, but ultimately turned it down because he did not want to do a visual-effects-heavy film directly after _[Titanic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")_.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Cast-Bullock-DiCaprio-19) The studio pushed for Reeves, who won the role over [Johnny Depp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johnny_Depp "Johnny Depp"), the Wachowskis' first choice.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Davis-interview-18) [Lorenzo di Bonaventura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lorenzo_di_Bonaventura "Lorenzo di Bonaventura") said the screenplay was also sent to [Sandra Bullock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandra_Bullock "Sandra Bullock"), with the suggestion of rewriting Neo as female.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Cast-Bullock-DiCaprio-19) - [Laurence Fishburne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laurence_Fishburne "Laurence Fishburne") as [Morpheus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morpheus_(The_Matrix) "Morpheus (The Matrix)"): A human freed from the Matrix and captain of the _Nebuchadnezzar_. Fishburne said that once he read the script, he did not understand why other people found it confusing. However, he doubted that the film would ever be made, because it was "so smart".[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) The Wachowskis instructed Fishburne to base his performance on the character [Morpheus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dream_(character) "Dream (character)") in [Neil Gaiman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman")'s _[Sandman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Sandman_(comic_book) "The Sandman (comic book)")_ comics.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-20) - [Carrie-Anne Moss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrie-Anne_Moss "Carrie-Anne Moss") as [Trinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinity_(The_Matrix) "Trinity (The Matrix)"): A human freed by Morpheus, a crewmember of the _Nebuchadnezzar_, and later Neo's romantic interest. After reading the script, Moss stated that at first, she did not believe she had to do the extreme acrobatic actions as described in the script. She also doubted how the Wachowskis would get to direct a movie with a budget so large, but after spending an hour with them going through the storyboard, she understood why some people would trust them.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) Moss mentioned that she underwent a three-hour physical test during casting, so she knew what to expect subsequently.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Training-21) The role launched Moss's acting career.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW2-22) [Janet Jackson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson") was initially approached for the role, but scheduling conflicts prevented her from accepting it.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-janet-23)[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-janet2-24) In an interview, Jackson stated that turning down the role was difficult for her, so she later referenced _The Matrix_ in the 'Intro' and 'Outro' interludes on her album _[Discipline](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discipline_(Janet_Jackson_album) "Discipline (Janet Jackson album)")_.[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-janet1-25) [Sandra Bullock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sandra_Bullock "Sandra Bullock"), who was previously approached for the role of Neo, was also offered the role of Trinity, but she turned it down.[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-26) [Rosie Perez](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rosie_Perez "Rosie Perez"), [Salma Hayek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salma_Hayek "Salma Hayek") and [Jada Pinkett Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jada_Pinkett_Smith "Jada Pinkett Smith") (who would later play [Niobe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Niobe_(The_Matrix) "Niobe (The Matrix)") in the sequels) also auditioned for the role.[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-27)[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-28)[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-29) - [Hugo Weaving](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Weaving "Hugo Weaving") as [Agent Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agent_Smith "Agent Smith"): A sentient "Agent" program of the Matrix whose purpose is to destroy Zion and stop humans from getting out of the Matrix. Unlike other Agents, he has ambitions to free himself from his duties. Weaving stated that he found the character amusing and enjoyable to play. He developed a neutral accent for Smith, wanting him to sound neither robotic nor human, and said that the Wachowskis' voices also influenced Smith's voice.[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Interrogation_Room-30) [Jean Reno](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Reno "Jean Reno") was offered the role, but declined, as he was unwilling to move to Australia for the production.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-31) - [Gloria Foster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gloria_Foster "Gloria Foster") as [The Oracle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Oracle_(The_Matrix) "The Oracle (The Matrix)"): A prophet who still resides in the Matrix, helping the freed humans with her foresight and wisdom. - [Joe Pantoliano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Pantoliano "Joe Pantoliano") as Cypher: Another human freed by Morpheus, and a crewmember of the _Nebuchadnezzar_, but one who regrets taking the red pill and seeks to be returned to the Matrix, later betraying the rebels to Agent Smith. Pantoliano had worked with the Wachowskis prior to appearing in _The Matrix_, starring in their 1996 film _[Bound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bound_(1996_film) "Bound (1996 film)")_. - [Marcus Chong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcus_Chong "Marcus Chong") as Tank: The "operator" of the _Nebuchadnezzar_ and Dozer's brother; they are both "natural" (as opposed to bred) humans, born outside of the Matrix. - [Paul Goddard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paul_Goddard_(actor) "Paul Goddard (actor)") as Agent Brown: One of two sentient "Agent" programs in the Matrix, who works with Agent Smith to destroy Zion and stop humans from escaping the system. - [Robert Taylor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Taylor_(Australian_actor) "Robert Taylor (Australian actor)") as Agent Jones: One of two sentient "Agent" programs in the Matrix who works with Agent Smith to destroy Zion and stop humans from escaping the system. - [Julian Arahanga](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julian_Arahanga "Julian Arahanga") as Apoc: A freed human and a crew member on the _Nebuchadnezzar_. - [Matt Doran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matt_Doran "Matt Doran") as Mouse: A freed human and a programmer on the _Nebuchadnezzar_. - [Belinda McClory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Belinda_McClory "Belinda McClory") as Switch: A human freed by Morpheus, and a crew member of the _Nebuchadnezzar_. - [Anthony Ray Parker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Ray_Parker "Anthony Ray Parker") as Dozer: Pilot of the _Nebuchadnezzar_. He is Tank's brother, and like him was born outside of the Matrix. - [Rowan Witt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rowan_Witt "Rowan Witt") as The Spoon Boy, a young prophet who has learnt how to manipulate the world of the Matrix. Seemingly wise beyond his years, he teaches Neo how to develop his powers and provides him with wisdom and motivation across the films and graphic novels. - [Ada Nicodemou](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ada_Nicodemou "Ada Nicodemou") as DuJour: A reference to the [White Rabbit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_Rabbit "White Rabbit") in _[Alice's Adventures in Wonderland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alice's_Adventures_in_Wonderland "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland")_. ## Production ### Development In 1994, the Wachowskis presented the script for the film _[Assassins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Assassins_(1995_film) "Assassins (1995 film)")_ to [Warner Bros. Pictures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"). After reading the script, [Lorenzo di Bonaventura](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lorenzo_di_Bonaventura "Lorenzo di Bonaventura"), the company's president of production at the time, decided to purchase it and included two more films, _[Bound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bound_(1996_film) "Bound (1996 film)")_ and _The Matrix_, in the contract. The Wachowskis first directed _Bound_, which became a critical success. Using this momentum, they asked to direct _The Matrix_.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) Keanu Reeves said that in an early draft of the script, "the Matrix avatar would be a different sex than the Zion reality", but he claimed the studio was not ready for that version.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-33) In 1996, the Wachowskis pitched the role of Neo to [Will Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Will_Smith "Will Smith"). Smith explained on his [YouTube](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel that the idea was for him to be Neo, while Morpheus was to be played by [Val Kilmer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Val_Kilmer "Val Kilmer"). He later explained that he did not quite understand the concept and he turned down the role to instead film _[Wild Wild West](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wild_Wild_West "Wild Wild West")_.[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-34) [Brad Pitt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt") also turned down the role of Neo.[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-35) [Madonna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") also turned down an undisclosed role, a decision she would later regret.[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-36) [Joel Silver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Silver "Joel Silver") soon joined the project as producer. Although the film had key supporters, including Silver and Di Bonaventura, _The Matrix_ was still a big gamble for Warner Bros., according to _[Screened](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whiskey_Media#Tested "Whiskey Media")_. The studio was investing $60 million in a script by a pair of relatively inexperienced directors who wanted to use complicated special effects that had never been achieved before.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) The Wachowskis therefore hired the underground comic book artists [Geof Darrow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geof_Darrow "Geof Darrow") and [Steve Skroce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steve_Skroce "Steve Skroce") to create a 600-page, shot-by-shot storyboard for the entire film.[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-37)[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-38)[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-39) The storyboard eventually earned the approval of Warner Bros., and the decision was made to film in Australia to make the most of the budget.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) Soon, _The Matrix_ became a co-production of Warner Bros. and [Village Roadshow Pictures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Village_Roadshow_Pictures "Village Roadshow Pictures").[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Museum-40) According to editor [Zach Staenberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zach_Staenberg "Zach Staenberg"), after the production team sent an edit to studio executives of the film's first few minutes—featuring Trinity's encounter with police and Agents—the project had "total support" from Warner Bros.[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-41) ### Pre-production The cast were required to be able to understand and explain the premise of _The Matrix_.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) French philosopher [Jean Baudrillard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Baudrillard "Jean Baudrillard")'s _[Simulacra and Simulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simulacra_and_Simulation "Simulacra and Simulation")_ was required reading for most of the principal cast and crew.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Simulacra-42) In early 1997, the Wachowskis had Reeves and Carrie-Anne Moss read _Simulacra and Simulation_, [Kevin Kelly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevin_Kelly_(editor) "Kevin Kelly (editor)")'s _[Out of Control: The New Biology of Machines, Social Systems, and the Economic World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Out_of_Control:_The_New_Biology_of_Machines,_Social_Systems,_and_the_Economic_World "Out of Control: The New Biology of Machines, Social Systems, and the Economic World")_, and [Dylan Evans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dylan_Evans "Dylan Evans")'s ideas on [evolutionary psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_psychology "Evolutionary psychology") before they read the film's script.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) Moss commented that she had difficulty with this process.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) The Wachowskis had long been admirers of [Hong Kong action cinema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema"), so they decided to hire the Chinese [martial arts choreographer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fight_choreography "Fight choreography") and film director [Yuen Woo-ping](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuen_Woo-ping "Yuen Woo-ping") to work on fight scenes. To prepare for the [wire fu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wire_fu "Wire fu"), the actors had to train rigorously.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) The Wachowskis initially scheduled four months of training, beginning in October 1997.[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-43) Yuen was at first optimistic about the training, but began to worry when he realized how unfit the actors were.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Training-21) Yuen focused on developing each actor's strength. He built on Reeves's diligence, Fishburne's resilience, Weaving's precision, and Moss's grace.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Training-21) He designed Moss's moves to suit her deftness and lightness.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Trinity-44) Prior to pre-production, Reeves underwent [spinal fusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spinal_fusion "Spinal fusion") surgery on his [cervical spine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cervical_vertebrae "Cervical vertebrae") to address [disc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intervertebral_disc "Intervertebral disc") problems that were causing him to lose his balance.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-45) He was still recovering by the time of pre-production, but he insisted on training, so Yuen let him practice punches and lighter moves. Reeves trained hard and even requested training on days off. However, he was unable to kick for two out of four months of training, and so did not kick much in the film.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Training-21)[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-46) Weaving had to undergo hip surgery after he sustained an injury during training.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) ### Filming [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Campbell_Street_train_bridge_at_intersection_of_Elizabeth_Street.jpg/500px-Campbell_Street_train_bridge_at_intersection_of_Elizabeth_Street.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Campbell_Street_train_bridge_at_intersection_of_Elizabeth_Street.jpg) Campbell Street train bridge at the intersection of Elizabeth Street was one of the filming locations in Sydney. All but a few scenes were filmed at [Fox Studios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disney_Studios_Australia "Disney Studios Australia") in Sydney, as well as in the city proper, although recognizable landmarks were not included to maintain the impression of a generic American city. The filming helped establish [New South Wales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") as a major film production center.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-47)[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-48) Filming began in March 1998 and wrapped in August 1998; [principal photography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") took 118 days.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Power_Plant-49) Some filming also occurred at [Culver Studios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Culver_Studios "Culver Studios").[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-50) Due to Reeves's neck injury (see above), some of the action scenes had to be rescheduled to wait for his full recovery. As a result, the filming began with scenes that did not require much physical exertion,[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Shooting_Begins-51)[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-52) such as the scene in Thomas Anderson's office, the interrogation room,[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Interrogation_Room-30) or the car ride in which Neo is taken to see the Oracle.[\[52\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Car_Ride_to_the_Oracle-53) Locations for these scenes included [Martin Place](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Martin_Place "Martin Place")'s fountain in Sydney, halfway between it and the adjacent Colonial Building, and the Colonial Building itself.[\[53\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-CNN_location-54) During the scene set on a government building rooftop, the team filmed extra footage of Neo dodging bullets in case the [bullet time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bullet_time "Bullet time") process did not work. The bullet-time fight scene was filmed on the roof of [Symantec](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gen_Digital "Gen Digital") Corporation building in Kent Street, opposite [Sussex Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sussex_Street,_Sydney "Sussex Street, Sydney").[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Government_Roof-55) Moss performed the shots featuring Trinity at the beginning of the film and performed all the wire stunts herself.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Trinity-44) The rooftop set that Trinity uses to escape from Agent Brown early in the film was left over from the production of _[Dark City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_City_(1998_film) "Dark City (1998 film)")_, which has prompted comments due to the thematic similarities of the films.[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-56) During the rehearsal of the lobby scene, in which Trinity runs on a wall, Moss injured her leg and was ultimately unable to film the shot in one take. She stated that she was under a lot of pressure at the time and was devastated when she realized that she would be unable to do it.[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Government_Lobby-57) The [dojo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dojo "Dojo") set was built well before the actual filming. During the filming of these action sequences, there was significant physical contact between the actors, earning them bruises. Reeves's injury and his insufficient training with wires before filming meant he was unable to perform the triple kicks satisfactorily and became frustrated with himself, causing the scene to be postponed. The scene was shot successfully a few days later, with Reeves using only three takes. Yuen altered the choreography and made the actors pull their punches in the last sequence of the scene, creating a training feel.[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Construct_Kung_Fu-58) The filmmakers originally planned to shoot the subway scene in an actual [subway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metro_station "Metro station"), but the complexity of the fight and related wire work required shooting the scene on a set. The set was built around an existing train storage facility, which had real train tracks. Filming the scene when Neo slammed Smith into the ceiling, [Chad Stahelski](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chad_Stahelski "Chad Stahelski"), Reeves's stunt double, sustained several injuries, including [broken ribs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rib_fracture "Rib fracture"), knees, and a [dislocated shoulder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dislocated_shoulder "Dislocated shoulder"). Another stuntman was injured by a hydraulic puller during a shot in which Neo was slammed into a booth.[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-El_Fighting-59) The office building in which Smith interrogated Morpheus was a large set, and the outside view from inside the building was a large, three story high [cyclorama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclorama "Cyclorama"). The helicopter was a full-scale, lightweight mock-up suspended by a wire rope operated by a tilting mechanism mounted to the studio roof beams. The helicopter had a real minigun side-mounted to it, which was set to cycle at half its regular (3,000 rounds per minute) firing rate.[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Helicopter_Rescue-60) To prepare for the scene in which Neo wakes up in a pod, Reeves lost 15 pounds (7kg) and shaved his whole body to give Neo an emaciated look. The scene in which Neo fell into the sewer system concluded the principal photography.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Power_Plant-49) According to _The Art of the Matrix_, at least one filmed scene and a variety of short pieces of action were omitted from the final cut of the film.[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-61) ### Sound effects and music See also: [The Matrix: Original Motion Picture Score](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Original_Motion_Picture_Score "The Matrix: Original Motion Picture Score") and [The Matrix: Music from the Motion Picture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Music_from_the_Motion_Picture "The Matrix: Music from the Motion Picture") [Dane A. Davis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dane_A._Davis "Dane A. Davis") was responsible for creating the sound effects for the film. The fight scene sound effects, such as the whipping sounds of punches, were created using thin metal rods and recording them, then editing the sounds. The sound of the pod containing a human body closing required almost fifty sounds put together.[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Sound_effects-62) The film's [score](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Film_score "Film score"), _[The Matrix: Original Motion Picture Score](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Original_Motion_Picture_Score "The Matrix: Original Motion Picture Score")_, was composed by [Don Davis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Don_Davis_(composer) "Don Davis (composer)").[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-63)[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-64) He noted that mirrors appear frequently in the film: reflections of the blue and red pills are seen in Morpheus's glasses; Neo's capture by Agents is viewed through the rear-view mirror of Trinity's motorcycle; Neo observes a broken mirror mending itself; reflections warp as a spoon is bent; the reflection of a helicopter is visible as it approaches a skyscraper. Davis focused on this theme of reflections when creating his score, alternating between sections of the orchestra and attempting to incorporate [contrapuntal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Counterpoint "Counterpoint") ideas. Davis' score combines orchestral, choral, and synthesizer elements; the balance between these elements varies depending on whether humans or machines are the dominant subject of a given scene.[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Score-65) In addition to Davis' score, [The Matrix: Music from the Motion Picture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Music_from_the_Motion_Picture "The Matrix: Music from the Motion Picture") also features music from acts such as [Rammstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rammstein "Rammstein"), [Rob Dougan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rob_Dougan "Rob Dougan"), [Rage Against the Machine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rage_Against_the_Machine "Rage Against the Machine"), [Propellerheads](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Propellerheads "Propellerheads"), [Ministry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ministry_(band) "Ministry (band)"), [Lunatic Calm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lunatic_Calm "Lunatic Calm"), [Deftones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deftones "Deftones"), [Monster Magnet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monster_Magnet "Monster Magnet"), [The Prodigy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Prodigy "The Prodigy"), [Rob Zombie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rob_Zombie "Rob Zombie"), [Meat Beat Manifesto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meat_Beat_Manifesto "Meat Beat Manifesto") and [Marilyn Manson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marilyn_Manson_(band) "Marilyn Manson (band)").[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-66)[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-67)[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-68) ### Production design See also: [Matrix digital rain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_digital_rain "Matrix digital rain") In the film, the code that composes the Matrix itself is frequently represented as downward-flowing green characters.[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-69) This code uses a custom typeface designed by Simon Whiteley,[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Museum-40) which includes mirror images of [half-width kana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Half-width_kana "Half-width kana") characters and Western Latin letters and Arabic numerals.[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Look_of_the_Matrix-70) In a 2017 interview at [CNET](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNET "CNET"), he attributed the design to his wife, who is from Japan, and added, "I like to tell everybody that The Matrix's code is made out of Japanese sushi recipes".[\[70\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-71) "The color green reflects the green tint commonly used on early [monochrome computer monitors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monochrome_monitor "Monochrome monitor")".[\[71\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-72) Lynne Cartwright, the Visual Effects Supervisor at [Animal Logic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_Logic "Animal Logic"), supervised the creation of the film's opening title sequence, as well as the general look of the Matrix code throughout the film, in collaboration with Lindsay Fleay and Justen Marshall.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Museum-40) The portrayal resembles the opening credits of the 1995 [Japanese cyberpunk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japanese_cyberpunk "Japanese cyberpunk") film, _[Ghost in the Shell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_in_the_Shell_(1995_film) "Ghost in the Shell (1995 film)")_, which had a strong influence on the _Matrix_ series. It was also used in the subsequent films, on the related website, and in the game _[The Matrix: Path of Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Path_of_Neo "The Matrix: Path of Neo")_, and its drop-down effect is reflected in the design of some posters for the _Matrix_ series. The code received the Runner-up Award in the 1999 Jesse Garson Award for In-film typography or opening credit sequence.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Museum-40) _The Matrix_'s [production designer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Production_design "Production design"), [Owen Paterson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Owen_Paterson_(production_designer) "Owen Paterson (production designer)"), used methods to distinguish the "real world" and the Matrix in a pervasive way. The production design team generally placed a bias towards the Matrix code's distinctive green color in scenes set within the simulation, whereas there is an emphasis on the color blue during scenes set in the "real world". In addition, the Matrix scenes' sets were slightly more decayed, monolithic, and grid-like to convey the cold, logical, and artificial nature of that environment. For the "real world", the actors' hair was less styled, their clothing had more textile content, and the cinematographers used longer lenses to soften the backgrounds and emphasize the actors.[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Look_of_the_Matrix-70) The _[Nebuchadnezzar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebuchadnezzar_(The_Matrix) "Nebuchadnezzar (The Matrix)")_ was designed to have a patched-up look, instead of clean, cold, and sterile spaceship interior sets as used on productions such as _[Star Trek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")_. The wires were made visible to show the ship's working internals, and each composition was carefully designed to convey the ship as "a marriage between Man and Machine".[\[72\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Nebuchadnezzar-73) For the scene when Neo wakes up in the pod connected to the Matrix, the pod was constructed to look dirty, used and sinister. During the testing of a breathing mechanism in the pod, the tester suffered hypothermia in under eight minutes, so the pod had to be heated.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Power_Plant-49) [Kym Barrett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kym_Barrett "Kym Barrett"), costume designer, said that she defined the characters and their environment by their costume.[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Costume-74) For example, Reeves's office costume was designed for Thomas Anderson to look uncomfortable, disheveled and out of place.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Shooting_Begins-51) Barrett sometimes used three types of fabric for each costume, and also had to consider the practicality of the acting. The actors needed to perform martial arts actions in their costumes, hang upside-down without people seeing up their clothing, and be able to work the wires while strapped into the harnesses.[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Costume-74) For Trinity, Barrett experimented with how each fabric absorbed and reflected different types of light, and was eventually able to make Trinity's costume mercury-like and oil-slick to suit the character.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Trinity-44) For the Agents, their costume was designed to create a secret service, undercover look, resembling the film _[JFK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JFK_(film) "JFK (film)")_ and classic [men in black](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Men_in_black "Men in black").[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Interrogation_Room-30) The sunglasses, a staple of the film's aesthetics, were commissioned for the film by designer Richard Walker from sunglasses maker Blinde Design.[\[74\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-75) ### Visual effects The " [bullet time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bullet_time "Bullet time")" effect was created for the film. A scene would be computer-modeled to decide the positioning of the physical cameras. The actor then provided their performance in a [chroma key](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chroma_key "Chroma key") setup, while the cameras were fired in rapid succession, with fractions of a second delay between each shot. The result was combined with CGI backgrounds to create the final effect at (0:33). > As for artistic inspiration for bullet time, I would credit [Otomo Katsuhiro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Otomo_Katsuhiro "Otomo Katsuhiro"), who co-wrote and directed _[Akira](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akira_(1988_film) "Akira (1988 film)")_, which definitely blew me away, along with director [Michel Gondry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michel_Gondry "Michel Gondry"). His music videos experimented with a different type of technique called view-morphing, and it was just part of the beginning of uncovering the creative approaches toward using still cameras for special effects. Our technique was significantly different because we built it to move around objects that were themselves in motion, and we were also able to create slow-motion events that 'virtual cameras' could move around—rather than the static action in Gondry's music videos with limited camera moves. — [John Gaeta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Gaeta "John Gaeta")[\[75\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-gaeta-empire-76) The film is known for popularizing a [visual effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visual_effect "Visual effect")[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Lane_City_of_God-77) known as " [bullet time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bullet_time "Bullet time")", which allows a [shot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shot_(filmmaking) "Shot (filmmaking)") to progress in [slow motion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slow_motion "Slow motion") while the camera appears to move through the scene at normal speed.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-guardian-sfx-78)[\[78\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-79) Bullet time has been described as "a visual analogy for privileged moments of consciousness within the Matrix",[\[79\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-80) and throughout the film, the effect is used to illustrate characters' exertion of control over time and space.[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-81) The Wachowskis first imagined an action sequence that slowed time while the camera pivoted rapidly around the subjects, and proposed the effect in their screenplay for the film. When [John Gaeta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Gaeta "John Gaeta") read the script, he pleaded with an effects producer at [Mass.Illusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manex_Visual_Effects "Manex Visual Effects") to let him work on the project, and created a prototype that led to him becoming the film's visual effects supervisor.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_VFX-82)[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-83) The method used for creating these effects involved a technically expanded version of an old art photography technique known as time-slice photography, in which an array of cameras is placed around an object and triggered simultaneously. Each camera captures a still picture, contributing one frame to the video sequence, which creates the effect of "virtual camera movement"; the illusion of a viewpoint moving around an object that appears frozen in time.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-guardian-sfx-78) The bullet time effect is similar but slightly more complicated, incorporating temporal motion so that, rather than appearing totally frozen, the scene progresses in slow and variable motion.[\[75\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-gaeta-empire-76)[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_VFX-82) The cameras' positions and exposures were [previsualized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Previsualization "Previsualization") using a 3D simulation. Instead of firing the cameras simultaneously, the visual effect team fired the cameras fractions of a second after each other, so that each camera could capture the action as it progressed, creating a super slow-motion effect.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-guardian-sfx-78) When the frames were put together, the resulting slow-motion effects reached a [frame frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frame_frequency "Frame frequency") of 12,000 per second, as opposed to the normal 24 frames per second of film.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) Standard movie cameras were placed at the ends of the array to pick up the normal speed action before and after. Because the cameras circle the subject almost completely in most of the sequences, computer technology was used to edit out the cameras that appeared in the background on the other side.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-guardian-sfx-78) To create backgrounds, Gaeta hired George Borshukov, who created 3D models based on the geometry of buildings and used the photographs of the buildings themselves as texture.\[ _[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\] The photo-realistic surroundings generated by this method were incorporated into the bullet time scene,[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_VFX-82) and algorithms based on [optical flow](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_flow "Optical flow") were used to interpolate between the still images to produce a fluent dynamic motion;[\[83\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-84)[\[84\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-85) the computer-generated "lead in" and "lead out" slides were filled in between frames in sequence to get an illusion of orbiting the scene.[\[85\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-86) Manex Visual Effects used a [cluster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cluster_(computing) "Cluster (computing)") farm running the [Unix-like](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") [operating system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") [FreeBSD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FreeBSD "FreeBSD") to render many of the film's visual effects.[\[86\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-87)[\[87\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-88) Manex also handled creature effects, such as Sentinels and machines in real-world scenes; [Animal Logic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal_Logic "Animal Logic") created the code hallway and the exploding Agent at the end of the film. DFilm managed scenes that required heavy use of digital compositing, such as Neo's jump off a skyscraper and the helicopter's crash into a building. The ripple effect in the latter scene was created digitally, but the shot also included practical elements, and months of extensive research were needed to find the correct kind of glass and explosives to use. The scene was shot by colliding a quarter-scale helicopter mock-up into a glass wall wired to concentric rings of explosives; the explosives were then triggered in sequence from the center outward, to create a wave of exploding glass.[\[88\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Post-production-89) The [photogrammetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photogrammetry "Photogrammetry") and image-based [computer-generated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") background approaches in _The Matrix_'s bullet time evolved into innovations unveiled in the sequels _[The Matrix Reloaded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")_ and _[The Matrix Revolutions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Revolutions "The Matrix Revolutions")_. The method of using real photographs of buildings as texture for 3D models eventually led the visual effect team to digitize all data, such as scenes, characters' motions, and expressions. It also led to the development of "Universal Capture", a process that samples and stores facial details and expressions at high resolution. With these highly detailed collected data, the team was able to create virtual cinematography in which characters, locations, and events can all be created digitally and viewed through virtual cameras, eliminating the restrictions of real cameras.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_VFX-82) ## Release ### Home media _The Matrix_ was released on [DVD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") and [Laserdisc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laserdisc "Laserdisc") in its original [aspect ratio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aspect_ratio "Aspect ratio") of [2.39:1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2.39:1 "2.39:1") on September 21, 1999, in the US from [Warner Home Video](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warner_Home_Video "Warner Home Video") as well as in [1.33:1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aspect_ratio_(image) "Aspect ratio (image)") aspect ratio in [Hong Kong](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") from ERA Home Entertainment. It was also released on [VHS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VHS "VHS") in both [full screen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fullscreen_(filmmaking) "Fullscreen (filmmaking)") and [widescreen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Widescreen "Widescreen") formats on December 7, 1999.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) After its DVD release, it was the first DVD to sell more than one million copies in the US.[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Million_sales-90) By 2000, the film went on to become the first to sell more than three million copies in the US.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) At that point, it became the top-selling DVD release of all time, holding this record for a few months before being surpassed by _[Gladiator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gladiator_(2000_film) "Gladiator (2000 film)")_.[\[90\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-91) By November 10, 2003, one month after _The Matrix Reloaded_ DVD was released, the sales of _The Matrix_ DVD had exceeded 30 million copies.[\[91\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-92) In the UK, the film debuted on both VHS and DVD formats on November 29, 1999\. _The Matrix_ sold more than 107,000 DVD copies in just two weeks, breaking _[Armageddon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")_'s record for being the country's best-selling DVD title.[\[92\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-93) [The Ultimate Matrix Collection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Ultimate_Matrix_Collection "The Ultimate Matrix Collection") was released on [HD DVD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD") on May 22, 2007,[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Million_sales-90) and on [Blu-ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") on October 14, 2008.[\[93\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-94)[\[94\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-DVD_Release_Date-95) The film was also released standalone in a 10th-anniversary edition Blu-ray in the Digibook format on March 31, 2009, ten years to the day after the film was released theatrically.[\[95\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-96) In 2010, the film had another DVD release along with the two sequels as _The Complete Matrix Trilogy_. It was also released on 4K HDR Blu-ray on May 22, 2018.[\[96\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-97) The film as part of _The Matrix Trilogy_ was released on [4K](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/4K_resolution "4K resolution") [Ultra HD Blu-ray](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultra_HD_Blu-ray "Ultra HD Blu-ray") on October 30, 2018.[\[97\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-98) ### Other media The franchise also contains four video games: _[Enter the Matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enter_the_Matrix "Enter the Matrix")_ (2003), which contains footage shot specifically for the game and chronicles events taking place before and during _[The Matrix Reloaded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")_;[\[98\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-99) _[The Matrix Online](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Online "The Matrix Online")_ (2004), an [MMORPG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MMORPG "MMORPG") which continued the story beyond _The Matrix Revolutions_;[\[99\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-100)[\[100\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-101) _[The Matrix: Path of Neo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix:_Path_of_Neo "The Matrix: Path of Neo")_ (2005), which focuses on Neo's journey through the trilogy of films;[\[101\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-102) and _[The Matrix Awakens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Awakens "The Matrix Awakens")_ (2021), an interactive technology demonstration developed by _[Epic Games](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games")_ using _[Unreal Engine 5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unreal_Engine_5 "Unreal Engine 5")_.[\[102\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-103) The franchise also includes _[The Matrix Comics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Comics "The Matrix Comics")_, a series of [comics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book") and short stories set in the world of _The Matrix_, written and illustrated by figures from the comics industry. Most of the comics were originally presented for free on the official _Matrix_ website;[\[103\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-comics-104) they were later republished, along with some new material, in two printed [trade paperback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_paperback_(comics) "Trade paperback (comics)") volumes, called _The Matrix Comics, Vol 1 and Vol 2_.[\[104\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-105) ## Reception ### Box office _The Matrix_ grossed $27.8 million during its opening weekend, as well as earning $37.4 million in its first five days.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ShowsMuscle-106) It surpassed _[Lost in Space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lost_in_Space_(film) "Lost in Space (film)")_ and _[Indecent Proposal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indecent_Proposal "Indecent Proposal")_ simultaneously for having the biggest April and [Easter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Easter "Easter") opening weekends.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ShowsMuscle-106) The film also had the second-highest opening weekend for a spring starter film, trailing only behind _[Liar Liar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liar_Liar "Liar Liar")_.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ShowsMuscle-106) Three years later in 2002, _The Matrix_'s records for having the largest April and Easter opening weekends would be taken by _[The Scorpion King](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Scorpion_King "The Scorpion King")_ and _[Panic Room](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panic_Room "Panic Room")_ respectively.[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-107)[\[107\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-108) Upon its opening, it had the highest opening weekend of any 1999 film until that point, easily topping _[Payback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Payback_(1999_film) "Payback (1999 film)")_.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ShowsMuscle-106) Additionally, this was the biggest opening weekend for a [Keanu Reeves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves") film since _[Speed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_(1994_film) "Speed (1994 film)")_ in 1994.[\[108\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-109) It would go on to rank number one at the box office during its first weekend, beating out _[Forces of Nature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forces_of_Nature_(1999_film) "Forces of Nature (1999 film)")_, _[10 Things I Hate About You](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/10_Things_I_Hate_About_You "10 Things I Hate About You")_, _[The Out-of-Towners](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Out-of-Towners_(1999_film) "The Out-of-Towners (1999 film)")_, _[Analyze This](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Analyze_This "Analyze This")_ and _[EDtv](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EDtv "EDtv")_.[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ShowsMuscle-106) The film would remain at the top of the box office for two weeks until it was overtaken by _[Life](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Life_(1999_film) "Life (1999 film)")_.[\[109\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-110) During its fourth weekend, _The Matrix_ briefly returned to the number one spot.[\[110\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-111)[\[111\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-112) The following week, the film would be displaced by _[Entrapment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entrapment_(film) "Entrapment (film)")_.[\[112\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-113) In the UK, _The Matrix_ earned $4.9 million in its first three days, ranking it as the third-highest opening weekend for a [Warner Bros.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures") film, behind _[Batman Forever](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Batman_Forever "Batman Forever")_ and _[Batman & Robin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Batman_&_Robin_(film) "Batman & Robin (film)")_, while also delivering the third-highest opening weekend of the year, after _[A Bug's Life](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A_Bug's_Life "A Bug's Life")_ and _[Notting Hill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notting_Hill_(film) "Notting Hill (film)")_.[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BigPix-114) The film grossed $1.8 million in Taiwan, making the third-highest opening there, behind _[Armageddon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Armageddon_(1998_film) "Armageddon (1998 film)")_ and _[The Lost World: Jurassic Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Lost_World:_Jurassic_Park "The Lost World: Jurassic Park")_.[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BigPix-114) Additionally, it managed to surpass _[Lethal Weapon 4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_4 "Lethal Weapon 4")_ for having the market's highest opening for Warner Bros.[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BigPix-114) It also surpassed _[Eraser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eraser_(film) "Eraser (film)")_ to secure the distributor's highest opening weekend in Germany, collecting $592,000.[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-BigPix-114) In its original run, the film earned $171,479,930 (37.0%) in the United States and Canada and $292,037,453 (63.0%) in other countries, for a worldwide total of $463,517,383.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) In [North America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_America "North America"), it went on to become the fifth highest-grossing film of 1999 and the highest-grossing R-rated film of that year. Worldwide, it was the fourth-highest-grossing film of the year, after _[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")_, _[The Sixth Sense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Sixth_Sense "The Sixth Sense")_ and _[Toy Story 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toy_Story_2 "Toy Story 2")_.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) _The Matrix_ became the second-highest-grossing Warner Bros. film of all time, behind _[Twister](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twister_(1996_film) "Twister (1996 film)")_.[\[114\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ResurrectionsHistory-115) Overall, it was the third-highest-grossing R-rated film at the time, just after _[Saving Private Ryan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saving_Private_Ryan "Saving Private Ryan")_ and _[Terminator 2: Judgment Day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terminator_2:_Judgment_Day "Terminator 2: Judgment Day")_.[\[114\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ResurrectionsHistory-115) Following re-releases, the worldwide gross of the film is $466,621,824.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) In 2012, it was placed 122nd on the list of highest-grossing films of all time, and the second-highest-grossing film in the _Matrix_ franchise after _The Matrix Reloaded_ ($742.1million).[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Mojo-6) ### Critical response _The Matrix_ was praised by many critics, as well as filmmakers, and authors of science fiction,[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-meta-8) especially for its "spectacular action" scenes and its "groundbreaking special effects". Some have described _The Matrix_ as one of the best [science fiction films](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") of all time;[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-heritage1-9)[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-autogenerated1-10) _[Entertainment Weekly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")_ called _The Matrix_ "the most influential action movie of the generation".[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW2-22) There have also been those, including philosopher [William Irwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Irwin_(philosopher) "William Irwin (philosopher)"), who have suggested that the film explores significant philosophical and spiritual themes. On [review aggregator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film holds an approval rating of 83% based on 210 reviews, with an average score of 7.7/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "Thanks to the Wachowskis' imaginative vision, _The Matrix_ is a smartly crafted combination of spectacular action and groundbreaking special effects".[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-RottenTomatoes-7) At [Metacritic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which assigns a rating out of 100 to reviews from mainstream critics, the film received a score of 73 based on 35 reviews, indicating "generally favorable reviews".[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-meta-8) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A−" on an A+ to F scale.[\[115\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-CinemaScore-116) It ranked 323rd among critics, and 546th among directors, in the [2012 _Sight & Sound_ polls of the greatest films ever made](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Sight_&_Sound_Top_50_Greatest_Films_of_All_Time "The Sight & Sound Top 50 Greatest Films of All Time").[\[116\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-117) Philip Strick commented in _[Sight & Sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sight_&_Sound "Sight & Sound")_, if the Wachowskis "claim no originality of message, they are startling innovators of method," praising the film's details and its "broadside of astonishing images".[\[117\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-sightandsound-118) [Roger Ebert](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") gave the film three stars out of four: he praised the film's visuals and premise, but disliked the [third act](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three-act_structure "Three-act structure")'s focus on action.[\[118\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-rogerebert-119) Similarly, _[Time Out](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_Out_Group "Time Out Group")_ praised the "entertainingly ingenious" switches between different realities, [Hugo Weaving](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hugo_Weaving "Hugo Weaving")'s "engagingly odd" performance, and the film's cinematography and production design, but concluded, "the promising premise is steadily wasted as the film turns into a fairly routine action pic... yet another slice of overlong, high-concept hokum."[\[119\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-timeout-120) [Jonathan Rosenbaum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jonathan_Rosenbaum "Jonathan Rosenbaum") of the _[Chicago Reader](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chicago_Reader "Chicago Reader")_ reviewed the film negatively, criticizing it as "simpleminded fun for roughly the first hour, until the movie becomes overwhelmed by its many sources ... There's not much humor to keep it all life-size, and by the final stretch it's become bloated, mechanical, and tiresome."[\[120\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Rosenbaum_review-121) Ian Nathan of _[Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empire_(magazine) "Empire (magazine)")_ described Carrie-Anne Moss as "a major find", praised the "surreal visual highs" enabled by the bullet time (or "flo-mo") effect, and described the film as "technically mind-blowing, style merged perfectly with content and just so damn cool". Nathan remarked that although the film's "looney plot" would not stand up to scrutiny, that was not a big flaw because " _The Matrix_ is about pure experience".[\[121\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Empire_review-122) [Maitland McDonagh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maitland_McDonagh "Maitland McDonagh") said in her review for _[TV Guide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")_, the Wachowskis' "through-the-looking-glass plot... manages to work surprisingly well on a number of levels: as a dystopian sci-fi thriller, as a brilliant excuse for the film's lavish and hyperkinetic fight scenes, and as a pretty compelling call to the dead-above-the-eyeballs masses to unite and cast off their chains... This dazzling pop allegory is steeped in a dark, pulpy sensibility that transcends nostalgic pastiche and stands firmly on its own merits."[\[122\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-TV_Guide_review-123) Joe Williams of _[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")_ earned a 3 out of 4 review, stating that "while the new sci-fi thriller _The Matrix_ won't win any prizes for its script or its acting, it may be the most outlandish feat of futurism since _[Blade Runner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blade_Runner "Blade Runner")_."[\[123\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-124) _[Salon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salon_(website) "Salon (website)")_'s reviewer Andrew O'Hehir acknowledged that although _The Matrix_ is in his view a fundamentally immature and unoriginal film ("It lacks anything like adult emotion... all this pseudo-spiritual hokum, along with the over-ramped onslaught of special effects—some of them quite amazing—will hold 14-year-old boys in rapture, not to mention those of us of all ages and genders who still harbor a 14-year-old boy somewhere inside"), he concluded, "as in _[Bound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bound_(1996_film) "Bound (1996 film)")_, there's an appealing scope and daring to the Wachowskis' work, and their eagerness for more plot twists and more crazy images becomes increasingly infectious. In a limited and profoundly geeky sense, this might be an important and generous film. The Wachowskis have little feeling for character or human interaction, but their passion for _movies_—for making them, watching them, inhabiting their world—is pure and deep."[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Salon_review-125) Filmmakers and science fiction creators alike generally took a complimentary perspective of _The Matrix_. [William Gibson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Gibson "William Gibson"), a key figure in cyberpunk fiction, called the film "an innocent delight I hadn't felt in a long time", and stated, "Neo is my favourite-ever science fiction hero, absolutely."[\[125\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-126) [Joss Whedon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joss_Whedon "Joss Whedon") called the film "my number one" and praised its storytelling, structure and depth, concluding, "It works on whatever level you want to bring to it".[\[126\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-127) [Darren Aronofsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darren_Aronofsky "Darren Aronofsky") commented, "I walked out of _The Matrix_... and I was thinking, 'What kind of science fiction movie can people make now?' The Wachowskis basically took all the great sci-fi ideas of the 20th century and rolled them into a delicious pop culture sandwich that everyone on the planet devoured."[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Aronofsky:-128) [M. Night Shyamalan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M._Night_Shyamalan "M. Night Shyamalan") expressed admiration for the Wachowskis, stating, "Whatever you think of _The Matrix_, every shot is there because of the passion they have! You can see they argued it out!".[\[128\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Shyamalan:-129) [Simon Pegg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simon_Pegg "Simon Pegg") said that _The Matrix_ provided "the excitement and satisfaction that _[The Phantom Menace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Phantom_Menace "The Phantom Menace")_ failed to inspire. _The Matrix_ seemed fresh and cool and visually breathtaking, making wonderful, intelligent use of CGI to augment the on-screen action, striking a perfect balance of the real and the hyperreal. It was possibly the coolest film I had ever seen."[\[129\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-simonpegg-130) [Quentin Tarantino](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") counted _The Matrix_ as one of his twenty favorite movies from 1992 to 2009.[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Tarantino:-131) [James Cameron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron") called it "one of the most profoundly fresh science fiction films ever made".[\[131\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-132) [Christopher Nolan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan") described it as "an incredibly palpable mainstream phenomenon that made people think, Hey, what if this isn't real?"[\[132\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-133) ## Accolades Main article: [List of accolades received by the Matrix franchise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_accolades_received_by_the_Matrix_franchise "List of accolades received by the Matrix franchise") _The Matrix_ received [Academy Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for [Best Film Editing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Film_Editing "Academy Award for Best Film Editing"), Best Sound Editing, [Best Visual Effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") and [Best Sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Sound "Academy Award for Best Sound").[\[133\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-134)[\[134\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-135) The filmmakers were competing against other films with established franchises, like _[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")_, yet they won all four of their nominations.[\[135\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Oscars2000-136)[\[136\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-oscars2-137) _The
