Matrix_ also received [BAFTA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") awards for [Best Sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Sound "BAFTA Award for Best Sound") and [Best Achievement in Special Visual Effects](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Special_Visual_Effects "BAFTA Award for Best Special Visual Effects"), in addition to nominations in the [cinematography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Cinematography "BAFTA Award for Best Cinematography"), [production design](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Production_Design "BAFTA Award for Best Production Design") and [editing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Editing "BAFTA Award for Best Editing") categories.[\[137\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-baftas-138) In 1999, it won [Saturn Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn_Awards "Saturn Awards") for [Best Science Fiction Film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn_Award_for_Best_Science_Fiction_Film "Saturn Award for Best Science Fiction Film") and [Best Direction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturn_Award_for_Best_Director "Saturn Award for Best Director").[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Saturn_Award-139) In February 2022, the film was named one of the five finalists for Oscars Cheer Moment as part of the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences")' " [Oscars Fan ## Thematic analysis > _The Matrix_ is arguably the ultimate [cyberpunk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") artifact. — [William Gibson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Gibson "William Gibson")[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-williamgibson-142) See also: [The Matrix (franchise) §Influences and interpretations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise)#Influences_and_interpretations "The Matrix (franchise)"), and [The Wachowskis §Personal lives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Wachowskis#Personal_lives "The Wachowskis") _The Matrix_ draws from and alludes to numerous cinematic and literary works, and concepts from mythology, religion and philosophy, including the ideas of [Buddhism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Christianity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Gnosticism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism"), [Hinduism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and [Judaism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").[\[142\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-143) ### Film and television The pods in which the machines keep humans have been compared to images in _[Metropolis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metropolis_(1927_film) "Metropolis (1927 film)")_, and the work of [M. C. Escher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/M._C._Escher "M. C. Escher").[\[143\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Luddism-144) A resemblance to the eerie worlds of Swiss artist [H. R. Giger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H._R._Giger "H. R. Giger") was also recognized.[\[144\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-145)[\[145\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-146) The pods can be seen in _[Welcome to Paradox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Welcome_to_Paradox "Welcome to Paradox")_ Episode 4 "News from D Street" from a 1986 short story[\[146\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-147) of the same name by [Andrew Weiner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Weiner_(writer) "Andrew Weiner (writer)") which aired on September 7, 1998, on the [SYFY Channel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syfy_(TV_channel) "Syfy (TV channel)") and has a remarkably similar concept. In this episode, the hero is unaware he is living in virtual reality until he is told so by "the code man," who created the simulation and enters it knowingly. The Wachowskis have described [Stanley Kubrick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s _[2001: A Space Odyssey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")_ as a formative cinematic influence, and as a major inspiration on the visual style they aimed for when making _The Matrix_.[\[147\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-ebert-wachowskis-148)[\[148\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-149)[\[149\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Kubrick-150) Reviewers have also commented on similarities between _The Matrix_ and other late-1990s films such as _[Strange Days](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strange_Days_(film) "Strange Days (film)")_, _[Dark City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_City_(1998_film) "Dark City (1998 film)")_ and _[The Truman Show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Truman_Show "The Truman Show")_.[\[b\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-155) The similarity of the film's central concept to a device in the long-running series _[Doctor Who](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")_ has also been noted. As in the film, the [Matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrix_(Doctor_Who) "Matrix (Doctor Who)") of that series (introduced in the 1976 serial _[The Deadly Assassin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Deadly_Assassin "The Deadly Assassin")_) is a massive computer system which one enters using a device connecting to the head, allowing users to see representations of the real world and change its laws of physics; but if killed there, they will die in reality.[\[154\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-156) The action scenes of _The Matrix_ were also strongly influenced by live-action films such as those of director [John Woo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Woo "John Woo").[\[155\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_PK_Dick-157) The martial arts sequences were inspired by _[Fist of Legend](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fist_of_Legend "Fist of Legend")_, a critically acclaimed 1995 martial arts film starring [Jet Li](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jet_Li "Jet Li"). The fight scenes in _Fist of Legend_ led to the hiring of Yuen as fight choreographer.[\[156\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-158)[\[157\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-159) The Wachowskis' approach to action scenes drew upon their admiration for [Japanese animation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anime "Anime") such as _[Ninja Scroll](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ninja_Scroll "Ninja Scroll")_ and _[Akira](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akira_(1988_film) "Akira (1988 film)")_.[\[158\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wachowski_transcript-160) Director [Mamoru Oshii](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mamoru_Oshii "Mamoru Oshii")'s 1995 animated film _[Ghost in the Shell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_in_the_Shell_(1995_film) "Ghost in the Shell (1995 film)")_ was a particularly strong influence;[\[158\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wachowski_transcript-160) producer [Joel Silver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Silver "Joel Silver") has stated that the Wachowskis first described their intentions for _The Matrix_ by showing him that anime and saying, "We wanna do that for real".[\[159\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-silver-anime-1-161)[\[160\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-silver-anime-2-162) [Mitsuhisa Ishikawa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitsuhisa_Ishikawa "Mitsuhisa Ishikawa") of [Production I.G](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Production_I.G "Production I.G"), which produced _Ghost in the Shell_, noted that the anime's high-quality visuals were a strong source of inspiration for the Wachowskis. He also commented, "...cyberpunk films are very difficult to describe to a third person. I'd imagine that _The Matrix_ is the kind of film that was very difficult to draw up a written proposal for to take to film studios". He stated that since _Ghost in the Shell_ had gained recognition in America, the Wachowskis used it as a "promotional tool".[\[161\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-south_bank_show-163) ### Literary works The film makes several references to [Lewis Carroll](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll")'s _[Alice's Adventures in Wonderland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alice's_Adventures_in_Wonderland "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland")_.[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Salon_review-125) Comparisons have also been made to [Grant Morrison](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grant_Morrison "Grant Morrison")'s comic series _[The Invisibles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Invisibles "The Invisibles")_, with Morrison describing it in 2011 as "(it) seemed to me (to be) my own combination of ideas enacted on the screen".[\[162\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-supergods-164) Comparisons have also been made between _The Matrix_ and the books of [Carlos Castaneda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlos_Castaneda "Carlos Castaneda").[\[163\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-165) _The Matrix_ belongs to the [cyberpunk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyberpunk "Cyberpunk") genre of science fiction, and draws from earlier works in the genre such as the 1984 novel _[Neuromancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuromancer "Neuromancer")_ by [William Gibson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Gibson "William Gibson").[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-williamgibson-142) For example, the film's use of the term "Matrix" is adopted from Gibson's novel,[\[164\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Neuromancer_Natali_interview-166) though [L. P. Davies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L._P._Davies "L. P. Davies") had already used the term "Matrix" fifteen years earlier for a similar concept in his 1969 novel _[The White Room](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_White_Room_(novel)?action=edit&redlink=1 "The White Room (novel)")_ ("It had been tried in the States some years earlier, but their 'matrix' as they called it hadn't been strong enough to hold the fictional character in place").[\[165\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-167) After watching _The Matrix_, Gibson commented that the way that the film's creators had drawn from existing cyberpunk works was "exactly the kind of creative cultural osmosis" he had relied upon in his own writing;[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-williamgibson-142) however, he noted that the film's [Gnostic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism") themes distinguished it from _Neuromancer_, and believed that _The Matrix_ was thematically closer to the work of science fiction author [Philip K. Dick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philip_K._Dick "Philip K. Dick"), particularly Dick's speculative _Exegesis_.[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-williamgibson-142) Other writers have also commented on the similarities between _The Matrix_ and Dick's work;[\[155\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Wired_PK_Dick-157)[\[166\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-168)[\[167\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-169) one example of such influence is a Philip K. Dick's 1977 conference, in which he stated: "We are living in a computer-programmed reality, and the only clue we have to it is when some variable is changed, and some alteration in our reality occurs".[\[c\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-174) ### Philosophy In _The Matrix_, a copy of [Jean Baudrillard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean_Baudrillard "Jean Baudrillard")'s philosophical work _[Simulacra and Simulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simulacra_and_Simulation "Simulacra and Simulation")_, which was published in French in 1981, is visible on-screen as ["the book used to conceal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concealing_objects_in_a_book "Concealing objects in a book") disks",[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Jamie_Allen-175)[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Simulacra-42) and Morpheus quotes the phrase "desert of the real" from it.[\[173\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Poole_Baudrillard-176) "The book was required reading"[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Jamie_Allen-175) for the actors prior to filming.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Simulacra-42)[\[174\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-177) However, Baudrillard himself said that _The Matrix_ misunderstands and distorts his work.[\[173\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Poole_Baudrillard-176)[\[175\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-178) Some interpreters of _The Matrix_ mention Baudrillard's philosophy to support their claim "that the \[film\] is an [allegory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") for contemporary experience in a heavily commercialized, media-driven society, especially in developed countries".[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Jamie_Allen-175) The influence of _[The Matrixial Gaze](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrixial_Gaze "The Matrixial Gaze")_, the philosophical-psychoanalytical concept of [Bracha L. Ettinger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bracha_L._Ettinger "Bracha L. Ettinger") on the archaic matrixial space that resists the field of simulacra,[\[176\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-179)[\[177\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-180)[\[178\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-181) "was brought to the public's attention through the writings of art historians such as [Griselda Pollock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Griselda_Pollock "Griselda Pollock") and film theorists such as Heinz-Peter Schwerfel".[\[179\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-182)[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Jamie_Allen-175) In addition to Baudrillard and Ettinger, the Wachowskis were also significantly influenced by [Kevin Kelly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevin_Kelly_(editor) "Kevin Kelly (editor)")'s _[Out of Control: The New Biology of Machines, Social Systems, and the Economic World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Out_of_Control:_The_New_Biology_of_Machines,_Social_Systems,_and_the_Economic_World "Out of Control: The New Biology of Machines, Social Systems, and the Economic World")_, and [Dylan Evans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dylan_Evans "Dylan Evans")'s ideas on [evolutionary psychology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_psychology "Evolutionary psychology").[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Screenplay-13) Philosopher [William Irwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Irwin_(philosopher) "William Irwin (philosopher)") suggests that the idea of the "Matrix" – a generated reality invented by malicious machines – is an allusion to [Descartes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Descartes "Descartes")' " [First Meditation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_Meditation "First Meditation")", and his idea of an [evil demon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evil_demon "Evil demon"). The Meditation hypothesizes that the perceived world might be a comprehensive illusion created to deceive us.[\[180\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Salon_philosophy-183) The same premise can be found in [Hilary Putnam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilary_Putnam "Hilary Putnam")'s [brain in a vat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain_in_a_vat "Brain in a vat") scenario proposed in the 1980s.[\[180\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Salon_philosophy-183) A connection between the premise of _The Matrix_ and [Plato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plato "Plato")'s [Allegory of the Cave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allegory_of_the_Cave "Allegory of the Cave") has also been suggested. The allegory is related to Plato's [theory of Forms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theory_of_Forms "Theory of Forms"), which holds that the true essence of an object is not what we perceive with our senses, but rather its quality, and that most people perceive only the shadow of the object and are thus limited to false perception.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) The philosophy of [Immanuel Kant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immanuel_Kant "Immanuel Kant") has also been claimed as another influence on the film, and in particular how individuals within the Matrix interact with one another and with the system. Kant states in his _[Critique of Pure Reason](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Critique_of_Pure_Reason "Critique of Pure Reason")_ that people come to know and explore our world through synthetic means (language, etc.), and thus, this makes it rather difficult to discern truth from falsely perceived views. This means people are their own agents of deceit, and so in order for them to know the truth, they must choose to openly pursue the truth. This idea can be examined in Agent Smith's monologue about the first version of the Matrix, which was designed as a human [utopia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Utopia "Utopia"), a perfect world without suffering and with total happiness. Agent Smith explains that, "it was a disaster. No one accepted the program. Entire crops \[of people\] were lost." The machines had to amend their choice of programming in order to make people subservient to them, and so they conceived the Matrix in the image of the world in 1999. The world in 1999 was far from a utopia, but still, humans accepted this over a suffering-free utopia. According to William Irwin, this is Kantian, because the machines wished to impose a perfect world on humans in an attempt to keep people content, so that they would remain completely submissive to the machines, both consciously and subconsciously, but humans were not easy to make content.[\[181\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-184) ### Religion and mythology Andrew Godoski sees allusions to [Christ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christ "Christ"), including Neo's " [virgin birth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miraculous_births "Miraculous births")", his doubt in himself, the prophecy of his coming, along with many other [Christian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") references.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) Amongst these possible allusions, it is suggested that the name of the character [Trinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinity_(The_Matrix) "Trinity (The Matrix)") refers to Christianity's doctrine of the [Trinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinity "Trinity").[\[182\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-185) It has also been noted that the character Morpheus paraphrases the Chinese [Taoist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taoist "Taoist") philosopher [Zhuangzi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zhuang_Zhou "Zhuang Zhou") when he asks Neo, "Have you ever had a dream, Neo, that you were so sure was real? What if you were unable to wake from that dream? How would you know the difference between the real world and the dream world?"[\[183\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-186) [Matrixism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matrixism "Matrixism") is a fan-based, possibly satirical religion created as "the matrix religion".\[ _[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\] ### Transgender themes Years after the release of _The Matrix_, both of the Wachowskis came out as [transgender](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") [women](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans_woman "Trans woman").[\[184\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-TransMasterpiece-187) The red pill has been likened to red [estrogen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estrogen "Estrogen") pills.[\[185\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Vulture-188) Morpheus's description of the Matrix creating a sense that something is fundamentally wrong, "like a splinter in your mind", has been likened to [gender dysphoria](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gender_dysphoria "Gender dysphoria").[\[185\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Vulture-188) In the original script, Switch was a woman in the Matrix and a man in the real world, but this idea was removed.[\[186\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-189) In a 2016 [GLAAD Media Awards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GLAAD_Media_Award "GLAAD Media Award") speech, Lilly Wachowski said: "There's a critical eye being cast back on Lana, and I's \[ _[sic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sic "Sic")_\] work through the lens of our transness. This is a cool thing because it's an excellent reminder that art is never static."[\[187\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-190) In 2020, Lilly said _The Matrix_ was intended as an allegory for gender transition, but that "the corporate world wasn't ready".[\[188\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-trans-metaphor-191) She said it was "all about the desire for transformation but it was all coming from a closeted point of view", but that she did not know "how present my transness was in the background of my brain" when the Wachowskis were writing it.[\[188\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-trans-metaphor-191) In an interview with _[Variety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")_ in 2020, Reeves said the idea that the Matrix was an allegory for transgender identity "wasn't introduced to me when we started for production on the films."[\[189\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-reeves'_awareness-192) ## Legacy ### Filmmaking Following _The Matrix_, films made abundant use of slow motion, spinning cameras, and, often, the [bullet time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bullet_time "Bullet time") effect of a character freezing or slowing down and the camera dollying around them.[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Lane_City_of_God-77) The ability to slow down time enough to distinguish the motion of bullets was used as a central gameplay mechanic of several video games, including _[Max Payne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Max_Payne_(video_game) "Max Payne (video game)")_, in which the feature was explicitly referred to as "bullet time".[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_BBC-193)[\[191\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-194) It was also the defining game mechanic of the game _[Superhot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superhot "Superhot")_ and its sequels. _The Matrix_'s signature special effect, and other aspects of the film, have been [parodied](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parody "Parody") numerous times,[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW2-22) in comedy films such as _[Deuce Bigalow: Male Gigolo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deuce_Bigalow:_Male_Gigolo "Deuce Bigalow: Male Gigolo")_ (1999),[\[192\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-195) _[Scary Movie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scary_Movie "Scary Movie")_ (2000),[\[193\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-196) _[Shrek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shrek "Shrek")_ (2001),[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_BBC-193) _[Kung Pow! Enter the Fist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kung_Pow!_Enter_the_Fist "Kung Pow! Enter the Fist")_ (2002),[\[194\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-197) _[Lastikman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lastikman_(2003_film) "Lastikman (2003 film)")_ (2003); _[Marx Reloaded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marx_Reloaded "Marx Reloaded")_ in which the relationship between Neo and Morpheus is represented as an imaginary encounter between [Karl Marx](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Leon Trotsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky");[\[195\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-198) and in video games such as _[Conker's Bad Fur Day](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conker's_Bad_Fur_Day "Conker's Bad Fur Day")_.[\[196\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-199) It also inspired films featuring a black-clad hero, a sexy yet deadly heroine, and bullets ripping slowly through the air;[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW2-22) these included _[Charlie's Angels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie's_Angels_(2000_film) "Charlie's Angels (2000 film)")_ (2000) featuring [Cameron Diaz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cameron_Diaz "Cameron Diaz") floating through the air while the cameras flo-mo around her; _[Equilibrium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equilibrium_(film) "Equilibrium (film)")_ (2002), starring [Christian Bale](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christian_Bale "Christian Bale"), whose character wore long black leather coats like Reeves' Neo;[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_BBC-193) _[Night Watch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Night_Watch_(2004_film) "Night Watch (2004 film)")_ (2004), a [Russian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russia "Russia") megahit heavily influenced by _The Matrix_ and directed by [Timur Bekmambetov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timur_Bekmambetov "Timur Bekmambetov"), who later made _[Wanted](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wanted_(2008_film) "Wanted (2008 film)")_ (2008), which also features bullets ripping through air; and _[Inception](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inception "Inception")_ (2010), which centers on a team of sharply dressed rogues who are able to enter other people's dreams by "wiring in". The original _[Tron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tron "Tron")_ (1982) paved the way for _The Matrix_, and _The Matrix_, in turn, inspired [Disney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disney "Disney") to make its own Matrix with a _Tron_ sequel, _[Tron: Legacy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tron:_Legacy "Tron: Legacy")_ (2010).[\[197\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW-200) Also, the film's lobby shootout sequence was recreated in the 2002 Indian action comedy _[Awara Paagal Deewana](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Awara_Paagal_Deewana "Awara Paagal Deewana")_.[\[198\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-201) ### Choreographers and actors _The Matrix_ had a strong effect on action filmmaking in Hollywood. The film's incorporation of [wire fu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wire_fu "Wire fu") techniques, including the involvement of [fight choreographer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fight_choreography "Fight choreography") [Yuen Woo-ping](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuen_Woo-ping "Yuen Woo-ping") and other personnel with a background in [Hong Kong action cinema](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_action_cinema "Hong Kong action cinema"), affected the approaches to fight scenes taken by some subsequent Hollywood action films,[\[199\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-xmen-202) moving them towards more Eastern approaches.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) The success of _The Matrix_ created high demand for those choreographers and their techniques from other filmmakers, who wanted fights of similar sophistication: for example, wire work was employed in _[X-Men](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-Men_(film) "X-Men (film)")_ (2000)[\[199\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-xmen-202) and _[Charlie's Angels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie's_Angels_(2000_film) "Charlie's Angels (2000 film)")_ (2000),[\[197\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW-200) and Yuen Woo-ping's brother [Yuen Cheung-yan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yuen_Cheung-yan "Yuen Cheung-yan") was choreographer on _[Daredevil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daredevil_(film) "Daredevil (film)")_ (2003).[\[200\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-203) _The Matrix_'s Asian approach to action scenes also created an audience for Asian action films such as _[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon")_ (2000) that they might not otherwise have had.[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_BBC-193) Chad Stahelski, who had been a stunt double on _The Matrix_ prior to directing Reeves in the [_John Wick_ series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wick "John Wick"), acknowledged the film's strong influence on the _Wick_ films,[\[201\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-204) and commented, " _The Matrix_ literally changed the industry. The influx of martial-arts choreographers and fight coordinators now makes more, and they are more prevalent and powerful in the industry than stunt coordinators. _The Matrix_ revolutionized that. Today, action movies want their big sequences designed around the fights."[\[202\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-205) [Carrie-Anne Moss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrie-Anne_Moss "Carrie-Anne Moss") asserted that prior to being cast in _The Matrix_, she had "no career". It launched Moss into international recognition and transformed her career; in a _[New York Daily News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")_ interview, she stated, " _The Matrix_ gave me so many opportunities. Everything I've done since then has been because of that experience. It gave me so much".[\[203\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-cany-206) The film also created one of the most devoted movie fan-followings since _[Star Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")_.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_EW2-22) The combined success of the _Matrix_ trilogy, the [_Lord of the Rings_ films](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings_(film_series) "The Lord of the Rings (film series)") and the [_Star Wars_ prequels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prequel_trilogy "Prequel trilogy") made Hollywood interested in creating trilogies.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_Screened-32) Stephen Dowling from the [BBC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BBC "BBC") noted that _The Matrix_'s success in taking complex philosophical ideas and presenting them in ways palatable for impressionable minds might be its most influential aspect.[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Influence_BBC-193) ### Cultural impact _The Matrix_ was also influential for its impact on [superhero films](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superhero_films "Superhero films"). John Kenneth Muir in _The Encyclopedia of Superheroes on Film and Television_ called the film a "revolutionary" reimagination of movie visuals, paving the way for the visuals of later superhero films, and credits it with helping to "make [comic-book](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comic-book "Comic-book") [superheroes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superheroes "Superheroes") hip" and effectively demonstrating the concept of "faster than a speeding bullet" with its bullet time effect.[\[204\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-207) Adam Sternbergh of _[Vulture.com](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vulture.com "Vulture.com")_ credits _The Matrix_ with reinventing and setting the template for modern superhero blockbusters, and inspiring the superhero renaissance in the early 21st century.[\[205\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-208) One popular quote, "Guns. Lots of guns,"[\[206\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-209)[\[207\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-210) spoken by Neo near the climax of the film, was incorporated into _[John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wick:_Chapter_3_%E2%80%93_Parabellum "John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum")_ as a line spoken by protagonist [John Wick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Wick_(character) "John Wick (character)"), also played by Reeves.[\[208\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-211)[\[209\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-212) The line was considered for use by screenwriters [Patrick Casey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patrick_Casey_(writer) "Patrick Casey (writer)") and [Josh Miller](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Josh_Miller_(filmmaker) "Josh Miller (filmmaker)") in the film _[Sonic the Hedgehog 3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_3_(film) "Sonic the Hedgehog 3 (film)")_, which starred Reeves as the voice of [Shadow the Hedgehog](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shadow_the_Hedgehog "Shadow the Hedgehog"), but it was ultimately cut from that film's final script.[\[210\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-213)[\[211\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-214) ### Modern reception In 2001, _The Matrix_ placed 66th in the [American Film Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute")'s " [100 Years...100 Thrills](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AFI's_100_Years...100_Thrills "AFI's 100 Years...100 Thrills")" list.[\[212\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-AFIThrills-215) In 2007, _[Entertainment Weekly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")_ called _The Matrix_ the best science-fiction piece of media for the past 25 years.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-Genre's_Best-11) In 2009, the film was ranked 39th on _[Empire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Empire_(magazine) "Empire (magazine)")_'s reader-, actor- and critic-voted list of "The 500 Greatest Movies of All Time".[\[213\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-216) _The Matrix_ was voted as the fourth best science fiction film in the 2011 list _Best in Film: The Greatest Movies of Our Time_, based on a poll conducted by [ABC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") and _[People](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/People_(American_magazine) "People (American magazine)")_. In 2012, the film was selected for preservation in the [National Film Registry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the [Library of Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") for being "culturally, historically, and aesthetically significant."[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-NFR-12) ### Red pill and blue pill Main article: [Red pill and blue pill](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_pill_and_blue_pill "Red pill and blue pill") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/c/cf/Screenshot_from_1990_film_Total_Recall%2C_which_scholars_connect_to_The_Matrix.%281999%29.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Screenshot_from_1990_film_Total_Recall,_which_scholars_connect_to_The_Matrix.(1999).png) Scene from the 1990 film _[Total Recall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_Recall_(1990_film) "Total Recall (1990 film)")_ where Dr. Edgemar ( [Roy Brocksmith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roy_Brocksmith "Roy Brocksmith")) explains that swallowing a red pill is a "symbol of your desire to return to reality" Historians of film note that the trope of a "red pill" as decisive in a return to reality made its first appearance in the 1990 film _[Total Recall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_Recall_(1990_film) "Total Recall (1990 film)")_, which has a scene where the hero (played by [Arnold Schwarzenegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger")) is asked to swallow a red pill to symbolize his desire to return to reality from a dream-like fantasy.[\[214\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-217)[\[215\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-218) The premise of _The Matrix_ has been repurposed for multiple [conspiracy theories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories") and [alt-right](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alt-right "Alt-right") fringe groups. For example, some online [men's rights groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Men's_rights "Men's rights") use the term "redpill" to mean men realizing that they are supposedly being subjugated by feminism.[\[216\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-219)[\[217\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-220) The term has been used in discussion forums for right-wing topics such as [Gamergate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamergate_(harassment_campaign) "Gamergate (harassment campaign)"), [white supremacy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_supremacy "White supremacy"), [incel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incel "Incel") subculture and [QAnon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QAnon "QAnon").[\[218\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-auto-221) As of 2021, the verb "pill" and suffix "-pilled" had entered more mainstream use and had come to mean developing a sudden interest in something.[\[218\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-auto-221) ### Sequels and adaptations Main article: [The Matrix (franchise)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_(franchise) "The Matrix (franchise)") The film's mainstream success led to the making of two sequels, _[The Matrix Reloaded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Reloaded "The Matrix Reloaded")_ and _[The Matrix Revolutions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Revolutions "The Matrix Revolutions")_, both directed by the Wachowskis. These were [filmed back-to-back](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Back_to_back_film_production "Back to back film production") in one [shoot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Production_(film) "Production (film)") and released on separate dates in 2003.[\[219\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-222) The first film's introductory tale is succeeded by the story of the impending attack on the human enclave of [Zion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zion_(The_Matrix) "Zion (The Matrix)") by a vast machine army.[\[220\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-223)[\[221\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Matrix_Reloaded_BBC_Review-224) The sequels also incorporate longer and more ambitious action scenes, as well as improvements in bullet time and other visual effects.[\[221\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Matrix_Reloaded_BBC_Review-224)[\[222\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-225) Also released was _[The Animatrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Animatrix "The Animatrix")_, a collection of nine animated short films, many of which were created in the same [Japanese animation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anime "Anime") style[\[223\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Animatrix_IGN_Review-226) that was a strong influence on the live action trilogy. _The Animatrix_ was overseen and approved by the Wachowskis, who only wrote four of the segments themselves but did not direct any of them; much of the project was developed by notable figures from the world of anime.[\[223\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-The_Animatrix_IGN_Review-226) In March 2017, Warner Bros. was in the early stages of developing a relaunch of the franchise with [Zak Penn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zak_Penn "Zak Penn") in talks to write a treatment and interest in getting [Michael B. Jordan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_B._Jordan "Michael B. Jordan") attached to star. According to _[The Hollywood Reporter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")_ neither the Wachowskis nor Joel Silver were involved with the endeavor, although the studio would like to get at minimum the blessing of the Wachowskis.[\[224\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-227) On August 20, 2019, Warner Bros. Pictures Group chairman [Toby Emmerich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toby_Emmerich "Toby Emmerich") officially announced that a fourth Matrix film was in the works, with [Keanu Reeves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keanu_Reeves "Keanu Reeves") and [Carrie-Anne Moss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carrie-Anne_Moss "Carrie-Anne Moss") set to reprise their roles as Neo and Trinity, respectively.[\[225\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-228) _[The Matrix Resurrections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix_Resurrections "The Matrix Resurrections")_ was released in theaters and on HBO Max on December 22, 2021. In September 2022, [Danny Boyle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Danny_Boyle "Danny Boyle") was announced to be directing and producing a live immersive dance production of the film, entitled _Free Your Mind_, which debuted in October 2023 at the _Aviva Studios_ in [Manchester](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), England.[\[226\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Matrix#cite_note-229) ## See also - [Emap](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emap "Emap"). p.109\. Honestly, I didn't think they could do it, it was too ambitious. I saw _Bound_ and I loved it. _The Matrix_ is exactly what they pitched, but they were designing those cameras to get those freeze-frames, and I was like, "If that doesn't work, the movie looks ridiculous." I didn't feel comfortable with the level of importance placed on that effect working properly.... That's probably the only one that I turned down that I shouldn't have, but when you see somebody do it like Keanu, you think, "Thank God." I don't think I was mature enough as an actor at that point to get out of the way and just let it be and allow the directors to make the movie. I would have been trying to make jokes. Now I would have loved to take a shot and see what I would have done with it, and I know now I could absolutely have been mature enough to get out of the way. But back then, I don't think I was. 014. As far as casting goes, Keanu Reeves wasn't Larry and Andy's first choice, Johnny Depp was their first choice for Neo. Warner Bros. was going for this thing where they didn't want Johnny Depp, they wanted Brad Pitt or Val Kilmer. They told Larry and Andy that if Brad Pitt would do the picture, they'd green-light it right then. After Kilmer and Brad Pitt said no, Warner Bros. was willing to consider Johnny Depp, so it sort of came down to between Johnny Depp and Keanu Reeves, who Warner Bros. was pushing. Keanu was always really tuned in to the concept, and that made a big difference for Larry and Andy. I think it was a brilliant choice. Gary Oldman was considered for the role of Morpheus at one point, and Samuel Jackson as well. For Trinity, I think they were generally looking at lesser-known names for that character. 018. We went to Sandy Bullock and said 'We'll change Neo to a girl.' \[Producer\] Joel Silver and I worked with Sandy on 'Demolition Man,' and she was and continues to be a very good friend of mine. The Matrix was the third in a cycle of movies to arrive in the late nineties with a strikingly similar theme. Like its predecessors from the previous year, Dark City and The Truman Show, it tells the story of a seemingly ordinary man who suddenly finds that his whole life is fake: he is trapped in an artificially created environment designed to keep him in submission. Like the heroes of those earlier movies, Keanu Reeves' Neo starts to realise that he is somehow special, and tries to escape the confines of his prison. 154. metadata.ogDescription: The Matrix is a 1999 science fiction action film written and directed by the Wachowskis. The first installment in the Matrix film series, it stars Keanu Reeves, Laurence Fishburne, Carrie-Anne Moss, Hugo Weaving, and Joe Pantoliano. It depicts a dystopian future in which humanity is unknowingly trapped inside the Matrix, a simulated reality created by intelligent machines. The plot follows the computer hacker Neo, who is recruited by Morpheus into a rebellion against the machines. Following the success of the Wachowskis' 1996 film Bound, Warner Bros. gave the directing duo the go-ahead to make The Matrix. The film's action scenes were influenced by anime and martial arts films, particularly fight choreography and wire fu techniques from Hong Kong action cinema. Other influences include Plato's cave, 1990's Telnet hacker communities, and William Gibson's cyberpunk novel Neuromancer. The Matrix popularized terms such as the red pill, as well as a novel visual effect known as "bullet time" in which a character's heightened perception is represented by allowing the action within a shot to progress in slow motion while the camera appears to move through the scene at normal speed. The Matrix opened in theaters in the United States on March 31, 1999, to widespread acclaim from critics, who praised its innovative visual effects, action sequences, cinematography, and entertainment value. The film was a box office success, grossing over $460 million on a $63 million budget. It became the highest-grossing Warner Bros. film of 1999 and the fourth-highest-grossing film of that year. The Matrix received Academy Awards for Best Visual Effects, Best Film Editing, Best Sound and Best Sound Effects Editing. It was also the recipient of numerous other accolades, including Best Sound and Best Special Visual Effects at the 53rd British Academy Film Awards. The Wachowskis were awarded Best Director and Best Science Fiction Film at the 26th Saturn Awards. The Matrix is considered to be among the greatest science fiction films of all time. In 2012, it was selected for preservation in the United States National Film Registry by the Library of Congress for being "culturally, historically, and aesthetically significant". The film's success led to two sequels by the Wachowskis, The Matrix Reloaded and The Matrix Revolutions, both released in 2003. The Matrix franchise was further expanded through the production of comic books, video games, and an animated anthology film, The Animatrix. The franchise has also inspired books and theories expanding on some of the religious and philosophical ideas alluded to in the films. A fourth film, The Matrix Resurrections, was directed by Lana Wachowski and was released in 2021.
